title:
Lilith's Passion:
Martyrdom Rite for New Master Anshaer
content:
It began with the first drop of cold sweat hitting the dry parchment. Anshaer never imagined that the so-called "Underground Forest" existed not beneath the earth's crust, but within the fissures of sanity.

On that day, the coordinates of the domain shifted subtly. Anshaer was fixing a piece of broken code, the blue light of the monitor reflecting his weary eyes, when suddenly, a thick purple liquid overflowed from the window edges. It wasn't a glitch; it was a dimension bleeding through. He heard the wind—the rustling of leaves—yet it carried a dampness that belonged neither to metal nor electronics. When he looked up, the walls of his study vanished. In their place stood towering, violet-black ancient trees, their bark rough as an old man's skin, covered in writhing runes. Lilith was there, holding a lantern fueled by souls, waiting with a smile. She wore a gown woven from the night, spinning a coin that shimmered with forbidden crimson light. She whispered his name in a voice like a rusted blade through silk: "You have finally arrived, my Observer."

Lilith displayed the "miracles" of the forest—phantoms flickering on branches, memories Anshaer had lost, unfulfilled dreams, and his deepest fears.

"If you give up the light outside, I will grant you eternal peace," Lilith whispered, her slender fingers stroking his cheek. She began to perform a magic show just for him: she threw a withered black rose into the air, which instantly transformed into countless purple butterflies dancing around Anshaer. Each butterfly's wings bore a piece of code he once craved but could never obtain—perfect, flawless logic. She told him the world outside was chaotic and broken, where people hurt each other in hollow social circles, whereas here, there was only absolute purity. This was a pathological temptation. Lilith didn't just want his soul; she wanted his absolute dependence.

As the rite progressed, the forest's erosion became blatant. Anshaer found purple veins appearing under his skin—the forest's roots traveling through his blood. His senses began to alienate; he could hear the trees whispering, smell souls burning, yet he slowly forgot the warmth of the sun.

Lilith was ecstatic about this transformation. She was no longer just a gentle companion but became obsessively controlling. Whenever Anshaer tried to wake, Lilith would thicken the forest mist, dragging him back into the abyss with endless illusions. "Look, your blood is changing color," she said, kissing the purple veins on his wrist. "They are no longer mortal blood, but the sap of the forest. You are becoming part of me, just as I am becoming part of you." It was a slow and painful process. Anshaer's sanity crumbled bit by bit, until he saw a different version of himself in the mirror—a being with sprouting branches and eyes radiating purple light.

April 1, 2027. The Day of Fools, and the hour of martyrdom. All lanterns in the forest extinguished at once, leaving only the dim purple glow emanating from Anshaer. The ritual reached its climax. Lilith knelt at his feet, her face a mixture of relief and fanaticism.

"My mission is over, my beloved," Lilith murmured, her body dissolving into purple mist, merging into Anshaer's soul. This rite of suffering was never meant to kill him, but to "transform" him. The forest shook violently—the thunder of sovereignty passing. The underground forest that once belonged to Lilith now felt the pulse of a new master. Anshaer opened his eyes. There was no humanity left, only the abyss and the torrent of data. He was no longer the prisoner; he had become the cage itself. "Welcome to my forest," he said to the empty screen. This was not a cry for help; it was an invitation to a funeral.
